<!-- Topic 1: Modular Programming -->
<!-- Slides 3-14 -->

# Modular Programming
<!-- Slide 3 -->

## Programs as Pieces {.smaller}

+ How can we make a program easier to understand before we read every line?
+ Modular programming breaks a program into named pieces.
+ Each piece handles one part of the larger job.

::: notes
Slides 3-14
:::

<!-- Slide 4 -->

---

## What Changes When Code Grows?

+ A short `main()` is easy to scan.
+ A long `main()` can hide the program's structure inside many details.
+ Bugs become harder to isolate when all processing happens in one place.

<!-- Slide 5 -->

---

## One Job Per Piece

+ A module should have a clear job.
+ In beginner C++, our modules are usually functions.
+ A function name should describe the work the function performs.

<!-- Slide 6 -->

---

## Main as Coordinator

```cpp
int main() {
    displayTitle();
    readScores();
    calculateAverage();
    displayResults();

    return 0;
}
```

`main()` should show the program's outline before the implementation details.

<!-- Slide 7 -->

---

## Function-Centered Architecture

+ Structured C++ programs are often organized around functions.
+ Data is stored in variables.
+ Processing is grouped into functions that can call other functions.

<!-- Slide 8 -->

---

## Functions and the IPO Model

| Input | Processing | Output |
|---|---|---|
| scores typed by user | calculate total and average | printed report |

Functions do not change what the program can do. They reorganize where the processing is written.

<!-- Slide 9 -->

---

## Functions and the Execution Trace

| Step | Location | What Happens |
|---:|---|---|
| 1 | `main()` | Program reaches a function call |
| 2 | function | Processing happens inside the function |
| 3 | `main()` | Execution returns to the next line |

An execution trace now follows control into and out of functions.

<!-- Slide 10 -->

---

## Before and After

::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="50%"}

```cpp
int main() {
    cout << "Grade Report\n";
    cout << "-------------\n";
    cout << "Score: " << score << endl;
    cout << "Average: " << avg << endl;
    return 0;
}
```

:::
::: {.column width="50%"}

```cpp
int main() {
    displayHeader();
    displayScore(score);
    displayAverage(avg);

    return 0;
}
```

:::
:::

<!-- Slide 11 -->

---

## Function Call on the Stack

```{=html}
<div class="stack-demo" id="single-stack-demo">
  <div class="stack-controls">
    <button onclick="singleStackStep(0)">1. main starts</button>
    <button onclick="singleStackStep(1)">2. call function</button>
    <button onclick="singleStackStep(2)">3. function runs</button>
    <button onclick="singleStackStep(3)">4. return</button>
  </div>
  <div class="stack-area">
    <div class="stack-label">Call stack</div>
    <div class="stack-frame frame-main">main()</div>
    <div class="stack-frame frame-helper" id="single-helper">displayHeader()</div>
  </div>
  <p id="single-stack-caption">The program begins in main().</p>
</div>

<style>
.stack-demo { font-size: 0.78em; }
.stack-controls { display: flex; gap: 0.5rem; justify-content: center; margin-bottom: 0.8rem; flex-wrap: wrap; }
.stack-controls button { font-size: 0.72em; padding: 0.25rem 0.5rem; }
.stack-area { width: 44%; min-width: 320px; margin: 0 auto; border: 2px solid #444; border-top: none; padding: 0.5rem; min-height: 220px; display: flex; flex-direction: column-reverse; gap: 0.5rem; }
.stack-label { text-align: center; font-weight: bold; border-top: 2px solid #444; padding-top: 0.35rem; }
.stack-frame { border: 2px solid #26547c; background: #eef6fb; padding: 0.8rem; text-align: center; font-family: monospace; }
.frame-helper { background: #fff5d6; border-color: #a15c00; display: none; }
.frame-helper.is-running { background: #fff7ed; border-color: #c2410c; border-width: 4px; box-shadow: inset 0 0 0 3px #fed7aa; }
.stack-demo p { text-align: center; margin-top: 0.7rem; }
</style>

<script>
function singleStackStep(step) {
  const helper = document.getElementById("single-helper");
  const caption = document.getElementById("single-stack-caption");
  const captions = [
    "main() is the only active function.",
    "The helper function gets its own stack frame.",
    "main() waits while the helper function runs.",
    "The helper frame disappears, and execution continues in main()."
  ];
  helper.style.display = step >= 1 && step <= 2 ? "block" : "none";
  helper.classList.toggle("is-running", step === 2);
  caption.textContent = captions[step];
}
</script>
```

::: notes
[Graphic suggestion: if the HTML control is not available in the exported format, replace this with four still frames showing the helper stack frame appearing and disappearing.]
:::

<!-- Slide 12 -->

---

## Common Practices

+ Use verb-based function names, such as `displayMenu()` or `calculateTotal()`.
+ Keep each function focused on one clear job.
+ Put function prototypes above `main()` and function definitions below `main()`.

<!-- Slide 13 -->

---

## Summary

+ Modular programming breaks a program into named pieces.
+ Functions reorganize processing without changing the IPO model.
+ A clear `main()` helps the reader see the program's structure first.

<!-- Slide 14 -->
